content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob invented a brand-new algorithm - but they can't decide what to name it!

Alice suggested the name SAS_ASA​, and Bob suggested the name SBS_BSB​. (SAS_ASA​ and SBS_BSB​ are both strings of lowercase English letters.)

However, Alice thinks Bob's naming sense is really bad - she'll only be happy if the name given to the algorithm is not close to SBS_BSB​ at all.

More specifically, Alice will be happy if and only if the algorithm's name **does not share any characters** with SBS_BSB​.

Similarly, Bob thinks Alice's naming sense is really bad, and will only be happy if the algorithm's name doesn't share any characters with SAS_ASA​.

Is there a way to name the algorithm (using only lowercase English letters) so that both Alice and Bob are happy?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and MMM — the length of SAS_ASA​ and the length of SBS_BSB​, respectively.
The second line contains the string SAS_ASA​.
The third line contains the string SBS_BSB​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `"YES"` if a valid name exists for the algorithm, and `"NO"` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: SAS_ASA​ and SBS_BSB​ contain only lowercase English letters, i.e, the characters `'a'` through `'z'`.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We have SA=dijkstraS_A = \texttt{dijkstra}SA​=dijkstra and SB=blossomS_B = \texttt{blossom}SB​=blossom.
They can name the algorithm queen\texttt{queen}queen for example - you may verify that it shares no characters with both SAS_ASA​ and SBS_BSB​.
Many other names are possible too - some examples are puff, yen, new\texttt{puff, yen, new}puff, yen, new.
Test case 222: It can be shown that no matter what the algorithm is named, either Alice or Bob will be unhappy.
Test case 333: One valid name for the algorithm is zzz\texttt{zzz}zzz.
